content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 9, 1988

Nancy A. Dillon, CMC
City Clerk
City of Thousand Oaks
401 West Hillcrest Drive
Thousand Oaks, CA 91360

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-88-048

Dear Ms. Dillon:

You have written seeking information regarding the workshop
and pending regulations concerning newsletters sent at public
expense by or on behalf of incumbent candidates for election.
This issue is governed by Government Code Section 89001 which
was recently amended to apply to local as well as state elected
officers.

I enclose copies of alternative versions of the regulation
to implement Government Code Section 89001. The Commission
will consider adoption of one of these versions at its April 5,
1988, meeting in Sacramento. Your comments are welcomed.

In response to your question, this letter will confirm that
the City of Thousand Oaks' newsletter would be subject to the
provisions of Government Code Section 89001, if it is sent to
200 or more households in a calendar month during the election
cycle for the city and any incumbents are seeking re-election.
The restrictions would also apply if one or more councilmembers
were currently a candidate for any other elective office,
local, state or federal.

I trust that this letter adequately responds to your
inquiry. Should you have further questions, or comments on the
proposed regulation, I may be reached at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Diane M. Griffiths

By: Robert E. Leideigh
Counsel, Legal Division

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

City of Thousand Oaks
OFFICE OF THE CITY CLERK
NANCY A. DILLON, CMC

January 20, 1988

California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804-0807

On October 22, 1987 I addressed the enclosed letter
to the Commission. As stated in that letter, I
would appreciate any additional information generated
in that workshop. I would also like a response to
the comments regarding the City of Thousand Oaks
Newsletter.

Thank you.

Sincerely,

Nancy A. Dillon, CMC
City Clerk

NAD:nb/4:48
enclosure

xc: Grant R. Brimhall, City Manager

City of Thousand Oaks

OFFICE OF THE CITY CLERK
NANCY A. DILLON, CMC

October 22, 1987

California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: Mass Mailings at Public Expense after Public
Official has Declared Candidacy
G.C. Section 89001
Workshop - October 29, 1987

The City of Thousand Oaks in 1986 created an information newsletter
to be sent to all residents.

The first newsletter (Volume I, No. 1 - enclosed) was scheduled to
be mailed during the summer of 1986. Due to the City Municipal
election process which started in August with the candidate filings,
the newsletter mailing was postponed until after the November
election. All five Councilmembers were equally represented in the
newsletter; however, two incumbents intended to file for re-election.

The second newsletter (Volume II, No. 1) was mailed Spring 1987 a
non-election year.

The third newsletter scheduled for late November is to be a tabloid
and printed by the local newspaper.

The City's goal is to release two newsletters per year.

18901. Prohibition on Newsletter or Mass Mailing

My interpretation of this section is that it would apply to the
Thousand Oaks Newsletter during the election process for City Council
candidates - even-numbered years August through November.

California Fair Political Practices Commission
October 22, 1987
Page 2

I would appreciate any additional information and confirmation re-
garding the Newsletter after your October 29th meeting.

Sincerely,

Nancy A. Dillon, CMC
City Clerk

NAD:nb/4:58

enclosure

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


January 27, 1988


Nancy A. Dillon, CMC
City Clerk
P.O. Box 1496
Thousand Oaks, CA  91360

Re: 88-048

Dear Ms. Dillon:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform
Act was received on January 27, 1988 by the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact Robert Leidigh, an attorney in
the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions,
or more information is needed, you should expect a response
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written
advice. If more information is needed, the person assigned to
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to
advise you as to information needed. If your request is for
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can.
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec.
18329).)

     You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

NAD:nb/4:58

enclosure

On the City Scene

Volume 1, No. 1                            City of Thousand Oaks                            Summer 1986

Five facilities on drawing board

Five major projects highlight the most significant Capital Improvements Program undertaken since City incorporation, according to Thousand Oaks Mayor Alex Fiore, outlining the planned $37.5 million investment.

Citizens of all ages and with varied needs will be served by several new facilities, a Senior Center, Teen Center, Human Services Center, and the East Valley Sheriff's Station. Expansion of the Thousand Oaks Civic Center will provide needed handicapped access.

"The facilities are in various stages of development. It's an ambitious program made possible because the City planned and saved...," said Fiore, who has served on the City Council since incorporation. He explained financial strategies undertaken to reach the goal; the tools of prudent cash management, systematic annual appropriation and saving of funds for designated projects have been utilized.

Approximately $10 million has been targeted for general improvements, $1.4

(See Projects, Page 3)

[IMAGE: Proposed Civic Center Addition]

Expansion program commences

Plans include handicapped access

A two-story addition to the City of Thousand Oaks Civic Center, part of the remodeling and expansion currently in progress will add needed space and refinements to the complex dedicated 13 years ago.

"The new facility will include a City Council chamber, enhanced handicapped access and a main entryway complete with a reception/information center," said City Councilman Lawrence E. Horner.

The initial facility was built to take care of the City's needs through 1985. Since its dedication, Feb. 24, 1973, the community's population has nearly doubled, going from approximately 50,000 to 97,000 residents.

"It was becoming necessary to rent office space that would meet City needs or construct an additional building," said Public Works Department Director Lou Suchera, in charge of the facility expansion project.

"That happens in the private sector also; recently our community newspaper expanded their offices, they simply outgrew the old building," Horner noted.

An additional 34,000 square feet will be added when the two-story structure between the existing and adjacent buildings is completed.

(See Civic Center, Page 4)

Teen, Senior Centers Set

Teens and seniors may soon find the empty acres next to the City of Thousand Oaks Library a thriving nucleus of activities. Within 12 to 18 months two separate centers, one for teens and one for seniors, could be open and operating at the Conejo Creek park site, says Councilman Lee Laxdal.

Facilities to serve both segments of the community have been long-standing priorities which are now being realized.

"Teens and seniors have specialized needs and we're working to meet them," says Mayor Pro Tem Frank Schillo, describing extensive efforts involving separate committees composed of City Councilmembers, Conejo Recreation and Park District representatives, and citizens. Schillo also serves on the Senior/Adult Center Committee. Laxdal chairs the Senior/Adult Center Committee and is a Teen Center Committee member.

(See Teen, Senior Centers, Page 3)

Citizens' Traffic Committee task: lending assistance

New structures are being built, but there's more to Thousand Oaks capital improvements than concrete and steel centers serving local citizens. The community's labyrinth of well-maintained streets, neatly manicured landscaping and other infrastructure elements are essential segments of a quality community, says City Councilwoman Madge Schaefer.

As Chair of the 15-member Citizen Traffic Committee, which identifies, discusses and seeks resolution to traffic concerns, Schaefer is well aware of the importance of street overlay programs, traffic signals, adequate water and wastewater facilities to a community.

(See Traffic, Page 4)

sections.question: You have written seeking information regarding the workshop and pending regulations concerning newsletters sent at public expense by or on behalf of incumbent candidates for election. Specifically, would the City of Thousand Oaks' newsletter be subject to the provisions of Government Code section 89001 during the election cycle when incumbents are seeking re-election?
sections.conclusion: In response to your question, this letter will confirm that the City of Thousand Oaks' newsletter would be subject to the provisions of Government Code section 89001, if it is sent to 200 or more households in a calendar month during the election cycle for the city and any incumbents are seeking re-election. The restrictions would also apply if one or more councilmembers were currently a candidate for any other elective office, local, state or federal.
sections.question_synthetic: The City of Thousand Oaks sought clarification on whether its citywide newsletter sent at public expense would be subject to Government Code section 89001 restrictions during election cycles when city council incumbents are candidates for re-election.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirmed that the City of Thousand Oaks newsletter is subject to Government Code section 89001 restrictions if sent to 200+ households monthly during election cycles when incumbents are seeking re-election, including candidacy for other offices.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document file contains multiple letters in sequence: incoming request (Oct 22, 1987), acknowledgment letter (Jan 27, 1988), follow-up from requester (Jan 20, 1988), and formal FPPC advice response (Feb 9, 1988). Primary response letter is dated February 9, 1988. Question and conclusion sections synthesized from content rather than formal section headers.

QUESTION: You have written seeking information regarding the workshop and pending regulations concerning newsletters sent at public expense by or on behalf of incumbent candidates for election. Specifically, would the City of Thousand Oaks' newsletter be subject to the provisions of Government Code section 89001 during the election cycle when incumbents are seeking re-election?

CONCLUSION: In response to your question, this letter will confirm that the City of Thousand Oaks' newsletter would be subject to the provisions of Government Code section 89001, if it is sent to 200 or more households in a calendar month during the election cycle for the city and any incumbents are seeking re-election. The restrictions would also apply if one or more councilmembers were currently a candidate for any other elective office, local, state or federal.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to the City of Thousand Oaks regarding whether its resident newsletter is subject to Government Code section 89001 restrictions on mass mailings at public expense during election cycles. The FPPC confirms the newsletter is subject to these restrictions when sent to 200+ households during election cycles with incumbent candidates seeking re-election.
